literal: three-natures, three-own-beings; from tri 'three' + svabhāva 'own-being, intrinsic nature'
invariant_definition: *Trisvabhāva* is the Yogācāra doctrine of three natures structuring every cognition. *Parikalpita-svabhāva* (imagined nature) — the falsely projected subject-object duality, which is what the doctrine declares non-existent. *Paratantra-svabhāva* (dependent nature) — the dependently-arisen cognitive flow itself, the substrate on which both the *parikalpita* deception and the *pariniṣpanna* realisation rest. *Pariniṣpanna-svabhāva* (consummate nature) — the *paratantra* purified of the *parikalpita*, which is the realised *tathatā*. The doctrine is the principal Yogācāra analytical apparatus and the central object of Mādhyamaka critique: Bhāviveka argues at length that the *paratantra* covertly reintroduces *svabhāva* — a real *paratantra*-cognition cannot itself be *niḥsvabhāva* — and the corresponding *triḥ-niḥsvabhāvatā* doctrine in the Yogācāra is the Yogācāra reply.

per_school.definition: The principal analytic apparatus of Asaṅga's *Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha* and Vasubandhu's *Triṃśikā* / *Trisvabhāva-Nirdeśa*. The *paratantra* is foregrounded as the load-bearing nature: it is the substrate of both deception (when overlaid with *parikalpita*) and realisation (when purified into *pariniṣpanna*). The magician-illusion analogy of the *Trisvabhāva-Nirdeśa* is the canonical illustration: a magician (*māyāvin*) creates the appearance of an elephant; the *parikalpita* is the apparent elephant; the *paratantra* is the *māyā*-substrate; the *pariniṣpanna* is the recognition that what appears as elephant is *māyā*.

Refused as covertly reintroducing *svabhāva* at the *paratantra* level: a real *paratantra*-cognition cannot itself be *niḥsvabhāva* and so the doctrine fails as a Mahāyāna alternative to Mādhyamaka. Bhāviveka's *Madhyamaka-Hṛdaya* chapter 5 develops the refutation at length; Candrakīrti's *Madhyamakāvatāra* chapter 6 supplies the Prāsaṅgika version.

The *trisvabhāva* analysis is rarely engaged by name in Vedāntic sources. The Vedāntic engagement with Yogācāra at Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.28–32 targets the *vijñapti-mātratā* thesis directly without working through the *trisvabhāva* apparatus. Yāmuna's *Saṃvit-Siddhi* engages the position similarly. Some structural parallels with Advaita's *adhyāsa* / *adhiṣṭhāna* / *brahman* triad have been noted in modern comparative scholarship, but the parallels are not load-bearing in classical Vedāntic exegesis.

translator_note: 'Three natures' is the standard gloss; 'three own-beings' would more literally translate *svabhāva* but reads awkwardly. The doctrine is widely misread as a Yogācāra metaphysics of three parallel layers of reality; it is more accurately read as three modes under which the same cognitive flow is to be analysed — *parikalpita* as how it falsely appears, *paratantra* as how it dependently arises, *pariniṣpanna* as how it is when correctly seen. The *triḥ-niḥsvabhāvatā* doctrine (each of the three is itself *niḥsvabhāva* in the Mahāyāna sense) is the Yogācāra resource for answering the Mādhyamaka critique.
